cue: Ask whether the value is in the set.
note: Set lookup is O(1) on average (constant time, no matter how many items) via hashing; scanning a list for the same check is O(n) (grows with the list length).

cue: Ask whether the value occurs anywhere in the list.
Track the size after adding the same value twice.
snippet:
s = set()
s.add(3)
s.add(3)
n = len(s)
n
note: A set stores each value once, so re-adding it is a no-op.

cue: One list of n flags, each starting at the same value.
note: [False] * n builds n separate slots; the next set stores presence by flipping one slot per key.

cue: Each key is one slot in the flag list; set it, read it, or clear it by index.
prompt: Complete `BoolSet` for keys in `0..n-1`, backed by a list of flags. Implement `add(k)`, `contains(k)`, and `remove(k)` without Python's built-in `set`.
tests:
s = BoolSet(5)
s.add(2)
s.add(2)
assert s.contains(2) is True, s.present
assert s.contains(0) is False, s.present
s.remove(2)
assert s.contains(2) is False, s.present
s.remove(4)
assert s.contains(4) is False, s.present
note: A set is one flag of presence per slot; indexing straight to the slot makes add, contains, and remove O(1).

concept: boolean-array set: contains reads the flag
cue: A set slot never touched is still its initial value.

concept: boolean-array set: remove clears the flag
cue: Removing a key just resets its slot.
snippet:
present = [False] * 5
present[3] = True
present[3] = False
present[3]

concept: hash a key into a bucket index
cue: Reduce the key's hash into the table's range with modulo.
note: A bucket is one slot of the backing array; hash(k) % nbuckets picks the slot. Keys that land in the same slot are chained (kept in a short list together).

concept: chaining hash set: add if absent
cue: Hash to a bucket, then guard against a duplicate before storing.
prompt: Complete `HashSet` (chaining over a fixed bucket array). Implement `add(k)` so a key is stored once and never duplicated. Do not use Python's built-in `set`.
tests:
s = HashSet()
s.add(5)
s.add(5)
s.add(1)
assert sum(len(b) for b in s.buckets) == 2, sum(len(b) for b in s.buckets)
note: Chaining means each bucket holds a short list of the keys that hashed to it. This is u04's HashMap with the value slot dropped: each bucket stores bare keys, not [key, val] pairs.

cue: Scan only the one bucket the key hashes to.
prompt: Add `contains(k)` to `HashSet`, returning whether key `k` is stored. Do not use Python's built-in `set`.
tests:
s = HashSet()
s.add(5)
assert s.contains(5) is True, 5
assert s.contains(9) is False, 9

cue: Locate the key's bucket, then drop the key only if it is there.
prompt: Add `remove(k)` to `HashSet`, unlinking key `k` from its bucket if present. Do not use Python's built-in `set`.
tests:
s = HashSet()
s.add(5)
s.add(1)
s.remove(5)
assert s.contains(5) is False, 5
assert s.contains(1) is True, 1
s.remove(99)
assert s.contains(1) is True, 1

concept: {} is a dict, not a set
cue: Consider which type the bare `{}` literal builds, dict or set.
note: {} is an empty dict; the empty set has no literal, so write set().

cue: Read each element by position: a[i] for i in range(len(a)).
snippet:
a = [4, 5, 6]
seen = set()
for i in range(len(a)):
    seen.add(a[i])
sorted(seen)

cue: Walk positions with range(len(a)); keep a seen set and append new values.
prompt: Write `dedup(a)` returning the distinct elements of list `a` in first-seen order. Iterate by index, not with a for-each loop.

cue: Build a set once, then walk targets by index and test each against it.
prompt: Write `is_member(items, targets)` returning a list of booleans, one per element of `targets`, indicating whether it appears in `items`.
note: Building the set costs O(n) once; each of the m queries is then O(1), for O(n+m) instead of O(n*m) rescans.

concept: dedup with for-each (shorthand for the indexed walk)
cue: The for-each loop `for x in a` is the shorthand for reading a[i] at each index.
prompt: Write `dedup(a)` returning the distinct elements of list `a` in first-seen order. Use a for-each loop `for x in a`.

cue: When a value returns, move it to the end instead of skipping it.
prompt: Write `dedup_last(a)` returning the distinct elements of list `a`, ordered by where each value LAST occurs. So a repeated value takes the position of its final appearance.

cue: Trace what the set keeps, then what sorting does to it.
Trace which order dict keys retain.
note: Unlike set(), dict keys keep first-seen insertion order, so this dedups without losing order.

cue: Walk positions with range(len(a)), bailing out the moment a[i] is seen twice.
prompt: Write `has_dup(a)` returning True if any element of list `a` appears more than once, else False. Stop as soon as a repeat is found.

cue: Compare the distinct count against the total count.
note: One line, but it always scans the whole list; the early-exit loop can stop at the first repeat.

cue: Keep only the elements found in both sets.
Gather every element from either set, without repeats.
Keep the left set's elements that are missing from the right.
Keep the elements that belong to exactly one set.

concept: intersection via filter against a set
cue: Put one list in a set, then filter the other against it.
syntax: sorted(x for x in xs if cond)
prompt: Write `common(a, b)` returning the sorted list of distinct values present in both list `a` and list `b`. Filter one set against the other by hand; do not use the intersection operator.

cue: Collect the distinct results the expression can produce.
Trace how discard behaves when the key is absent.
note: discard removes the value if present and does nothing if absent; remove would raise KeyError here.

concept: sliding window: shrink from the left on a repeat
cue: The window holds a, b, c; the incoming char repeats one already inside, so drop chars from the left until it is gone.
snippet:
s = "abcb"
seen = {'a', 'b', 'c'}
l = 0
while 'b' in seen:
    seen.discard(s[l])
    l += 1
(sorted(seen), l)
note: This is one shrink step of the sliding window: when the incoming char already sits in the window, remove from the left until it no longer does. The next write repeats this each time the right edge moves.

cue: Grow a window on the right, shrinking from the left when a char repeats.
prompt: Write `longest_unique(s)` returning the length of the longest substring of string `s` with no repeated character. Use a sliding window backed by a set.
note: One pass is O(n): each index joins and leaves the window's set once, instead of re-checking every substring.
mapsTo: LC 3 · Longest Substring Without Repeating Characters
